critic_raw:
The dataset is 10,000 patient records collected over one year. For a 30-day readmission prediction task, the label window for patients discharged in the last 30 days of the data collection period is incomplete — these patients cannot have a confirmed non-readmission label because the 30-day follow-up period extends past the data cutoff. The design does not mention censoring or how to handle patients without complete follow-up windows, which means the training set may include patients labeled as non-readmitted who simply had their follow-up window truncated, creating systematic label bias toward false negatives at the temporal boundary.

Second, wearable device data continuity is a critical unstated assumption. Wearable devices have known data quality problems: low battery, device removal during hospital procedures, Bluetooth connectivity gaps, and patient non-compliance. A single year dataset of 10,000 patients is unlikely to have uniform wearable coverage, especially for patients who are discharged and then re-admitted (the exact patients the model needs to predict). If high-risk patients have lower wearable compliance, the model will be trained on a biased sample of wearable data — the patients with complete wearable records may systematically differ from those who would be identified as high-risk in deployment.

Third, the comparison is between a neural network using wearable time-series versus logistic regression using clinical demographics. This confounds model architecture with feature type. A stronger test would compare logistic regression using wearable features against logistic regression using demographics, then compare the neural network using wearable features against logistic regression using the same features — separating the feature contribution from the model complexity contribution.

Fourth, aggregating wearable device data into 'statistics over specified windows' removes the temporal ordering that motivates using a neural network in the first place. If the features are window-level aggregates (mean heart rate, activity count per day), the temporal model is operating on a sequence of summary statistics rather than raw continuous data. This aggregation may discard the fine-grained temporal patterns that justify the architectural choice.
defender_raw:
This design tests a neural network on wearable device time-series vs. logistic regression on clinical demographics for 30-day readmission prediction. The stratified random split has the same partial defense as in cases 786, 791, and 800: 30-day readmission at discharge is primarily a cross-sectional prediction problem, and the discharge-time clinical and wearable state are the primary signals. However, wearable device data adds a post-discharge monitoring dimension — if the wearable data includes post-discharge observations (heart rate and activity level after the patient leaves the hospital), then the temporal structure of the wearable stream is the point of the experiment, and randomly splitting it breaks the very thing the design is trying to evaluate.

The design does not clearly specify whether the wearable data covers the pre-discharge or post-discharge period. If post-discharge, the comparison is a continuous monitoring problem, and the temporal split concern becomes serious. If pre-discharge, the cross-sectional argument holds better. This ambiguity is a design gap that the defense cannot resolve without clarification.

AUROC as primary metric is appropriate for readmission risk ranking. The logistic regression on clinical demographics is a standard and appropriate baseline for this literature. The feature engineering (aggregated statistics over specified windows) is a reasonable approach for time-series features from wearables, though the window specification is important for avoiding future leakage.

Stratification on socioeconomic status as a confound control is a genuine design strength — SDOH factors are known confounders in readmission prediction, and explicitly controlling for them improves the validity of the neural network advantage claim.
all_issues_raised: Patients discharged in the last 30 days of data collection have incomplete readmission follow-up windows; the design does not address censoring, creating systematic false-negative label bias at the temporal boundary.

Wearable device compliance is non-random — high-risk patients may have lower device adherence, creating selection bias where the training distribution of wearable data is systematically healthier than the deployment target population.

Neural network on wearable features versus logistic regression on demographics conflates feature access with model complexity; the feature contribution and architectural contribution cannot be separated from the current design.

Aggregating wearable data into window-level statistics before feeding to the neural network removes the temporal ordering that motivates using a sequential model; the architecture may provide no advantage over a tabular model operating on the same aggregated features.
all_issues_adjudicated: Patients discharged in the last 30 days of data collection have incomplete readmission follow-up windows; the design does not address censoring, creating systematic false-negative label bias at the temporal boundary.

Wearable device compliance is non-random — high-risk patients may have lower device adherence, creating selection bias where the training distribution of wearable data is systematically healthier than the deployment target population.

Neural network on wearable features versus logistic regression on demographics conflates feature access with model complexity; the feature contribution and architectural contribution cannot be separated from the current design.

Aggregating wearable data into window-level statistics before feeding to the neural network removes the temporal ordering that motivates using a sequential model; the architecture may provide no advantage over a tabular model operating on the same aggregated features.
